pois.name: Hamburger Rathaus
pois.description: The seat of the government of Hamburg, built in the Neo-Renaissance style with an ornate sandstone facade and a 112-meter high tower. It houses 647 rooms and sits on over 4,000 oak piles.

pois.name: Hauptkirche St. Michaelis
pois.description: Commonly known as 'Michel', this is Hamburg's most famous landmark and one of the finest Hanseatic Protestant baroque churches. Its distinctive copper roofed tower served as a beacon for ships.

pois.name: Speicherstadt
pois.description: The world's largest warehouse district where buildings stand on timber-pile foundations. This UNESCO World Heritage site features Gothic Revival brick architecture and narrow canals.

pois.name: Chilehaus
pois.description: An exceptional example of Brick Expressionism, this office building resembles a ship's prow. It was designed by Fritz Höger and is part of the UNESCO-listed Kontorhausviertel.

pois.name: Mahnmal St. Nikolai
pois.description: The burnt-out spire and crypt of this former church serve as a memorial to the victims of war and tyranny. It was once the tallest building in the world in the late 19th century.

pois.name: Schloss Bergedorf
pois.description: The only surviving castle within the Hamburg city limits, originally a medieval water castle. It served as the seat of the bailiffs and now houses a regional history museum.

pois.name: Alter Elbtunnel
pois.description: Also known as the St. Pauli Elbe Tunnel, this underwater passage was an engineering marvel when it opened in 1911. It features decorative terra cotta tiles and car elevators.

pois.name: Krameramtsstuben
pois.description: A historic cluster of 17th-century half-timbered houses built for the widows of members of the Grocers' Guild. It represents one of the last remaining examples of old Hamburg courtyards.

pois.name: Deichstraße
pois.description: The oldest remaining street in the Altstadt, featuring traditional half-timbered townhouses. This area is where the Great Fire of 1842 broke out.

pois.name: Hauptkirche St. Petri
pois.description: The oldest parish church in Hamburg, located at the city's highest point. The current Gothic building was reconstructed after the Great Fire of 1842.

pois.name: Hauptkirche St. Jakobi
pois.description: A medieval church famous for its Arp Schnitger organ, one of the most important baroque organs in Europe. It stands along the historic pilgrim route to Santiago de Compostela.

pois.name: Hauptkirche St. Katharinen
pois.description: A landmark for sailors and merchants, this church served the historic parish of the harbor. Its baroque tower was rebuilt after destruction in World War II.

pois.name: Altonaer Rathaus
pois.description: The former town hall of the city of Altona, a white Neo-Renaissance building with a prominent view over the Elbe river. It reflects Altona's independent history before joining Hamburg.

pois.name: Treppenviertel Blankenese
pois.description: A picturesque residential district on the slopes of the Elbe, characterized by narrow winding stairs and historic fishermen's houses.

pois.name: Friedhof Ohlsdorf
pois.description: The world's largest rural cemetery, serving as a massive parkland with historic mausoleums, sculptures, and burial sites of famous residents.

pois.name: Rickmer Rickmers
pois.description: A historic three-masted iron windjammer built in 1896, now serving as a museum ship and a permanent fixture of the Landungsbrücken harbor front.

pois.name: Köhlbrandbrücke
pois.description: An iconic cable-stayed bridge spanning the Köhlbrand branch of the Elbe, a key landmark of the industrial port and a masterpiece of 1970s engineering.

pois.name: Sternwarte Bergedorf
pois.description: The Hamburg Observatory is a significant ensemble of architectural and scientific history, featuring various telescope domes in a park-like setting.

pois.name: Harburger Schloss
pois.description: The remnants of a Renaissance palace built on the site of an earlier fortress. It was once the residence of the Dukes of Brunswick-Lüneburg.

pois.name: Bischofsburg
pois.description: Archaeological remains of a 11th-century bishop's residence located in a basement near the cathedral site, showing early medieval stone foundations.

pois.name: Ellerntorsbrücke
pois.description: Built in 1668, this is one of the oldest stone bridges in Hamburg, crossing the Herrengrabenfleet and formerly serving as a main route to Altona.

pois.name: Lombardsbrücke
pois.description: The bridge separating the Binnenalster from the Außenalster, named after a former pawn shop (Lombard) that stood nearby in the 17th century.

pois.name: Jungfernstieg
pois.description: Hamburg's premier urban promenade along the Binnenalster. It was the first street in Germany to be asphalted in 1838.

pois.name: Israelitischer Tempel Poolstraße
pois.description: The ruins of a reform synagogue built in 1844, serving as a reminder of the once flourishing Jewish life in the Neustadt district.

pois.name: Jüdischer Friedhof Altona
pois.description: A Sephardic and Ashkenazi cemetery established in 1611, known for its elaborately carved gravestones and deep historical significance for the Jewish diaspora.

pois.name: Fischauktionshalle
pois.description: A historic fish auction hall built in the style of a Roman basilica with a magnificent iron dome, still used for events and Sunday markets.

pois.name: Große Freiheit
pois.description: A famous side street of the Reeperbahn known for its religious freedom in the 17th century and later as the site of the Star-Club where the Beatles performed.

pois.name: Mahnmal Bullenhuser Damm
pois.description: A memorial located in a former school building dedicated to 20 Jewish children murdered here by the SS in the final days of World War II.

pois.name: KZ-Gedenkstätte Neuengamme
pois.description: The site of the largest concentration camp in Northwest Germany. The memorial preserves the brick factory where prisoners were forced into labor.

pois.name: Trostbrücke
pois.description: A historic bridge crossing the Nikolaifleet, decorated with statues of Count Adolf III and Bishop Ansgar, representing the secular and clerical sides of the city.

pois.name: Alter Schwede
pois.description: A massive glacial erratic boulder found in the Elbe riverbed, weighing 217 tons. It was transported from Småland during the Elster glaciation.

pois.name: Bismarck-Denkmal
pois.description: The world's largest statue of Otto von Bismarck, standing 34 meters tall in the Elbpark. It contains a complex system of underground catacombs.

pois.name: Jenisch Haus
pois.description: A classicist country villa located in Jenisch Park, reflecting the lifestyle of wealthy Hanseatic merchants in the 19th century.

pois.name: Kanzleigebäude Altona
pois.description: A baroque administrative building that survived the heavy bombing of Altona, once serving the Danish administration of the city.

pois.name: Komponistenquartier
pois.description: A cluster of museums in the Peterstraße dedicated to famous composers born or active in Hamburg, like Brahms and Mendelssohn, in reconstructed townhouses.

pois.name: Fleetinsel
pois.description: A historic area surrounded by canals (Fleete) in the city center, known for its galleries and preserved maritime atmosphere.

pois.name: Oberhafenbrücke
pois.description: A double-decker railway and road bridge with a distinctive steel structure, characteristic of Hamburg's early 20th-century port infrastructure.

pois.name: Alsterfontäne
pois.description: A large water fountain in the center of the Binnenalster lake, shooting water up to 60 meters high, serving as a symbol of the city's water wealth.

pois.name: Hanseatisches Oberlandesgericht
pois.description: A monumental courthouse building at Sievekingplatz, reflecting the judicial pride of the Free and Hanseatic City of Hamburg.

pois.name: Bornplatzsynagoge Site
pois.description: A ground-level mosaic showing the floor plan of the largest synagogue in Northern Germany, which was destroyed during the November Pogroms in 1938.

pois.name: Leuchtturm Neuwerk
pois.description: The oldest building in Hamburg, located on an island in the North Sea. This tower served as a defense fortification and lighthouse since the 14th century.

pois.name: Leuchtturm Bunthaus
pois.description: A small wooden lighthouse located at the fork of the Norderelbe and Süderelbe rivers, marking the entrance to the Hamburg port area.

pois.name: Falkensteiner Ufer
pois.description: A scenic stretch of the Elbe riverbank in Blankenese with sandy beaches and a view of the passing container ships and the Mühlenberger Loch.

pois.name: Römischer Garten
pois.description: An Italian-style terraced garden situated on a high bank above the Elbe, offering a Mediterranean atmosphere and classical theater performances.

pois.name: Hauptbahnhof Hamburg
pois.description: Opened in 1906, it is Germany's busiest railway station. The platform hall is covered by a massive 73-meter wide steel and glass roof.

pois.name: Bahnhof Dammtor
pois.description: A magnificent example of Art Nouveau station architecture, once used as a ceremonial station for visits by the German Emperor.

pois.name: Holsten-Brauerei (Historischer Standort)
pois.description: The site of one of Hamburg's most traditional breweries, founded in 1879, which became a global brand and a cornerstone of Altona's industry.

pois.name: Gröninger Privatbrauerei
pois.description: One of the oldest breweries in the city center, maintaining a rustic cellar atmosphere where beer has been brewed since the late 18th century.

pois.name: Aalspeicher
pois.description: A traditional fish restaurant located in a historic warehouse on the Nikolaifleet, known for its preservation of old Hamburg culinary traditions.

pois.name: Gasthaus 'Zum Elfmeter'
pois.description: A historic harbor pub in the Veddel district, preserving the working-class social history of the port's industrial boom.

pois.name: Eichbaumsee
pois.description: A large recreational lake in the Marschlande area, created from gravel excavation for motorway construction, now a nature and swimming area.

pois.name: Hummelsbütteler Moore
pois.description: A protected peat bog area in the north of Hamburg, representing the original landscape before the city's expansion.

pois.name: Alte Harburger Elbbrücke
pois.description: A historic arched steel bridge for pedestrians and cyclists, formerly the first road bridge to cross the Elbe at Harburg.

pois.name: St. Johannis-Kloster
pois.description: Originally a Dominican monastery founded in the 13th century, it was later converted into a Protestant home for noble ladies; the current buildings are 19th-century Neo-Gothic.

pois.name: Schwebefähre (Osten-Hemmoor Site)
pois.description: While the famous transporter bridge is outside Hamburg, the city maintains historical ties to such engineering on its lower Elbe routes.

pois.name: Dockland
pois.description: An arrow-shaped office building that leans over the Elbe. Its public roof terrace offers panoramic views of the harbor and Altona.

pois.name: Flakturm IV Heiligengeistfeld
pois.description: One of the few remaining massive anti-aircraft bunkers from WWII. It stands as a grim reminder of air defense and now houses media companies.

pois.name: Elbphilharmonie
pois.description: A modern concert hall built atop a historic warehouse (Kaispeicher A). The 'Plaza' deck offers 360-degree views of the city and port.

pois.name: Gänsemarkt
pois.description: A historic square in the city center where the first public opera house outside Italy once stood. It features a monument to Gotthold Ephraim Lessing.

pois.name: Rieck Haus
pois.description: An open-air museum in Curslack featuring a traditional Low German farmhouse from 1533, showcasing the rural life of the Vierlande region.
